generatedBy: migration-issue-audit workflow (65 agents) + lead ground-truth/parity sweep
baseline: client 3352 tests + server 112 tests green pre-fix

confirmed.title: trustProxy:true + un-sanitizing nginx config lets clients spoof req.ip and fully bypass the per-IP auth rate limit
confirmed.description: The last migration commit (fbe7d1d) flipped Fastify to `trustProxy: true` so the per-IP auth rate-limit (the brute-force / enumeration / scrypt-DoS defense, max 10/min in app.ts:22-25) buckets on `req.ip`. With trustProxy:true, Fastify/proxy-addr derives `req.ip` from the LEFTMOST X-Forwarded-For entry, which is fully attacker-controlled. The prescribed deployment (server/deploy/README.md step 5) is `location /api/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:5180; }` — it does NOT replace or sanitize X-Forwarded-For, so a client header passes through (or is appended leftmost). An attacker who rotates the XFF header per request gets a unique req.ip each time, so the rate-limit bucket never accumulates and the limit is completely bypassed. This re-opens unlimited password brute-force and account-enumeration against /api/auth/login and /api/auth/signup, and enables the scrypt event-loop DoS (each call ~100ms blocking). The default @fastify/rate-limit keyGenerator is `req.ip` (node_modules/@fastify/rate-limit/index.js:29), so nothing else mitigates it. The added test (app.test.ts) actually demonstrates the spoof — it drives the bucket purely via an attacker-supplied X-Forwarded-For header and asserts that as correct behavior, cementing the vulnerable design.
confirmed.evidence:
app.ts:13  const app = Fastify({ logger: false, trustProxy: true });

Verified live: Fastify({trustProxy:true}) with header 'X-Forwarded-For: attacker-spoof-9.9.9.9' yields req.ip === 'attacker-spoof-9.9.9.9' (leftmost, client-controlled). Default keyGenerator: node_modules/@fastify/rate-limit/index.js:29 `const defaultKeyGenerator = (req) => req.ip`. Prescribed proxy (deploy/README.md): `location /api/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:5180; }` sets no X-Forwarded-For of its own.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Do not use unconditional `trustProxy: true`. Set trustProxy to the exact number of trusted hops in front of the app (e.g. `trustProxy: 1` for a single nginx) OR to the nginx loopback address, so Fastify ignores client-supplied XFF beyond the trusted proxy. In tandem, make the prescribed nginx config use `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr;` (replace, not the appending `$proxy_add_x_forwarded_for`) so the upstream value is overwritten with the real peer. Update app.test.ts to drive the bucket via the actual connection IP, not a raw client XFF header.

confirmed.title: scryptSync runs synchronously on the shared event loop — every login/signup freezes all WebSocket game traffic for ~100ms
confirmed.description: hashPassword and verifyPassword use the blocking `scryptSync` (not the async `scrypt`), and are called directly inside the /api/auth/signup and /api/auth/login route handlers (routes.ts:36, 53, 55). The server is a single Fastify process on a single event loop (index.ts: one buildApp, no cluster/worker) that ALSO serves the authoritative game WebSocket and its periodic state-push ticks (game/ws.ts). Measured on this box, one scryptSync(N=32768,r=8,p=1) call blocks the event loop ~100ms. So each auth attempt stalls every connected player's WS ticks and intent processing for ~100ms; a burst of concurrent logins (10 = the rate-limit ceiling, and unbounded once the XFF bypass above is used) produces ~1s+ of fully frozen game traffic. This is a regression introduced by the migration choosing the sync API on the request path of a process that also carries real-time game traffic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Switch to the async `crypto.scrypt` (promisified) in hashPassword/verifyPassword so the KDF runs on libuv's threadpool and does not block the event loop while game WS ticks for other players are pending. (Optionally raise UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE if many concurrent auths are expected.) Keep the same PHC encoding and timingSafeEqual comparison.

confirmed.title: Offline catch-up calls advanceIsland with no RatesContext — biome/NC/solar/toxicity/lattice all ignored, diverging from online play
confirmed.description: loadAndCatchUp integrates the offline gap with `advanceIsland(state, now)` — passing NEITHER the `ctx: RatesContext` NOR `wallClockNowMs` arguments. The client's economy loop (src/main.ts:2195-2204, 2282-2303) always builds a full RatesContext per island: `modifierMul` (biome multipliers), `ncBuff`, `activeBonusMul`, `terrainAt`, `solarBoost` (Mirror Sat), `geothermalActive`, cross-island lattice pooling, cable-network brownout, and `worldSeed`. With ctx omitted, computeRates falls back to identity defaults (economy.ts:1068-1071 modifierMul=IDENTITY, ncBuff=1, activeBonusMul=1; ctx?.solarBoost??0; ctx?.geothermalActive??false) and advanceIsland's `if (ctx?.worldSeed)` guard (economy.ts:2555) skips advanceToxicityRolls entirely. Concrete effect on a FRESH world (which already ships islands with modifiers — world.ts:613 fertile, 626 mineral_rich, 665 cursed_storms): per biomes.ts:292-295 fertile = extraction ×1.50, mineral_rich = ×1.25, cursed_storms = global ×0.90. So a fertile island under-produces offline by 33%, while a cursed_storms island OVER-produces offline (logging off dodges the storm penalty — an exploit). Buildings accrue ZERO toxicity offline. Lattice chains that depend on cross-island supply stall offline though they run online. This breaks SPEC §15.5 ('weather/toxicity rolls deterministic at any catchup duration'), §2003/§2133 ('offline play and active play the same code path'), and §15.6's authoritative-state contract.
confirmed.evidence:
runtime.ts:27  `if (state) advanceIsland(state, now);`  // no ctx, no wallClockNowMs
vs client main.ts:2282-2303 ctx includes `modifierMul: modifierMulFor(s.id), ncBuff: ncBuffFor(s), world: worldState, worldSeed: worldState.seed, solarBoost: ...`
economy.ts:2555 `if (ctx?.worldSeed) { advanceToxicityRolls(...) }`  // skipped server-side
biomes.ts:292-295  mineral_rich→×1.25, fertile→×1.50, cursed_storms→global×0.90
confirmed.suggestedFix: Mirror the client's advanceEconomy: in loadAndCatchUp build the same per-island RatesContext (modifierMul via effectiveModifierMultipliers, ncBuff from computeNcState, activeBonusMul, terrainAt, solarBoost, geothermalActive, worldSeed=world.seed) and the lattice/cable group handling, then pass it (plus wallClockNowMs=now) to advanceIsland — ideally by extracting the client's advanceEconomy into a shared pure module so server and client cannot drift.

confirmed.title: Concurrent intents from two connections of the same account silently lose updates (no DB lock / transaction / version check)
confirmed.description: applyIntent does a non-atomic read-modify-write: loadAndCatchUp (which itself loads+saves) → handler.apply mutates the in-memory game → saveSnapshot, across three separate awaits with NO row lock, SELECT…FOR UPDATE, advisory lock, transaction, or optimistic schema_version check (grep for FOR UPDATE/pg_advisory/BEGIN/transaction in server/src/game returns nothing). ws.ts:85-90 serializes intents per CONNECTION via a promise chain and its comment claims this 'prevents two intents from racing the same saves row' — but that guarantee is per-connection only. Two browser tabs (two WS connections) for the same user have independent chains and interleave freely against the single `saves` row keyed by user_id (0002_saves.sql:3). Same hazard between an intent on one connection and the 1 Hz periodic state-push tick on another (ws.ts:100, also write-via-loadAndCatchUp). I PROVED this empirically: a test firing two place-building intents at different tiles concurrently via Promise.all both ACK ok:true, yet the stored save contains only ONE workshop (expected 2, received 1) — the later writer clobbers the earlier mutation.
confirmed.evidence:
intent-runner.ts:41-64  `const game = await loadAndCatchUp(...); ... handler.apply(...); ... await saveSnapshot(...)`  — three awaits, no lock.
Empirical: two concurrent applyIntent place-building calls → `AssertionError: expected 1 to be 2` (one workshop persisted, one lost), both acks ok:true.
ws.ts:87 comment: 'This is what prevents two intents from racing (load->apply->persist) the same row.' (only true within one connection)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make load-apply-persist atomic per account: wrap the whole sequence in a single DB transaction that does SELECT snapshot … FOR UPDATE on the saves row (or take pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(user_id))), then UPDATE within the same tx; alternatively add an optimistic version column bumped per write and retry on conflict. Per-connection chaining is insufficient for multi-tab.

confirmed.title: POST /api/game/import persists fully client-authored snapshot as authoritative — contradicts the 'client numbers are never trusted' contract; arbitrary level/inventory and unbounded offline catch-up
confirmed.description: The import route validates a client-supplied snapshot only for STRUCTURE (isValidSaveSnapshot checks types + that v ∈ SUPPORTED_LOAD_VERSIONS — persistence.ts:1233-1242) and that it deserializes, then stores it verbatim as the account's authoritative save. There is no bound on any value (grep for level/inventory/clamp/MAX in routes.ts returns nothing): a logged-in player can POST a snapshot granting themselves any island level, any inventory, completed endgame artifacts, etc. Additionally `savedAt` is attacker-controlled and unbounded (isValidSaveSnapshot only checks `typeof savedAt === 'number'`); loadAndCatchUp computes deltaMs = max(0, now - snapshot.savedAt) (persistence.ts:932), so importing a snapshot with savedAt far in the past triggers a multi-year offline catch-up granting a large extra resource/XP bolus on top of the already-arbitrary state. This directly contradicts SPEC Appendix C ('Validation re-runs the existing pure entry function … client numbers are never trusted'). The slice-5 trust-surface doc (commit 2f111c2) audited XP/trade/casts but did not flag the import endpoint.
confirmed.evidence:
routes.ts:28-39  `if (!isValidSaveSnapshot(snapshot)) {...400...} try { deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), Date.now()); } ... await saveSnapshot(pool, userId, snapshot);`
persistence.ts:1236-1239 isValidSaveSnapshot: only `typeof v['savedAt'] !== 'number'` / `typeof v['v'] !== 'number'` shape checks — no value bounds.
runtime.ts:20 + persistence.ts:932 `deltaMs = Math.max(0, nowWallMs - snapshot.savedAt)` → unbounded offline integrate from attacker savedAt.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Treat import as untrusted: clamp savedAt to [now - MAX_OFFLINE_WINDOW, now] before persisting (or normalize savedAt/savedAtPerf to now on import so no offline bonus is granted), and either bound/sanitize imported state against legality rules or restrict import to a server-signed export. At minimum document and bound the offline window so import cannot mint arbitrary catch-up.

confirmed.title: Read paths (GET /api/game/state and 1 Hz WS state push) issue a DB write on every call via loadAndCatchUp
confirmed.description: loadAndCatchUp unconditionally calls saveSnapshot at the end, so every read-only operation persists. GET /api/game/state (routes.ts:45) and the periodic WS state push that runs every STATE_PUSH_INTERVAL_MS=1000 (ws.ts:41,100) both go through loadAndCatchUp, meaning each connected socket writes the full jsonb snapshot to Postgres once per second even when the player does nothing, and a plain state GET mutates persistent storage. Beyond write amplification (a large late-game snapshot serialized + UPSERT per second per socket), every such write widens the lost-update race window in finding #2: the read-triggered re-persist of advanced state is exactly what a concurrent intent on another connection can clobber or be clobbered by.
confirmed.evidence:
runtime.ts:29-30  `const advanced = serializeWorld(world, islandStates, now, now); await saveSnapshot(pool, userId, advanced);`  (always, even for reads)
ws.ts:41  pushState → `const game = await loadAndCatchUp(pool, userId, Date.now());`  fired by setInterval(…,1000) at ws.ts:100
routes.ts:45  state route → `const game = await loadAndCatchUp(pool, req.user!.id, Date.now());`
confirmed.suggestedFix: Split catch-up from persistence: have a read-only path that deserializes + advances in memory for projection/push without writing, and persist only on accepted mutations (or throttle/debounce the catch-up write, e.g. only when elapsed since last save exceeds a threshold). Combine with the transactional fix in #2.

confirmed.title: POST /api/game/import accepts an unvalidated client snapshot as authoritative state — full anti-cheat bypass
confirmed.description: The migration's whole purpose (SPEC Appendix C trust-surface) is to make the server authoritative so a client can never fabricate state. The import endpoint defeats this: it persists a fully client-supplied snapshot as the account's authoritative game, gated ONLY by `hasSave` (409 if a game already exists) and by STRUCTURAL validity (`isValidSaveSnapshot` + `deserializeWorld` not throwing). It performs ZERO legality validation of the CONTENT — inventory quantities, populated flags, skill points, building tiers, T5 endgame state are all taken verbatim. Any fresh account can therefore POST a hand-crafted snapshot with maxed inventory / all islands populated / all skills unlocked and the server will treat it as legitimate from then on. Every per-intent legality re-check in intents.ts (the anti-cheat work of this slice) is moot once a player can seed arbitrary starting state. The SPEC documents the mechanism (§ Appendix C, lines 2253-2257) as a 'one-time local-save migration' but does not acknowledge that it is an unauthenticated-content trust hole.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Treat imported content as untrusted: either (a) drop the import path and migrate local saves through replayed intents, or (b) sanitize on import — reject any snapshot whose state isn't reachable from a fresh game (e.g. clamp/zero inventories above plausible caps, reject pre-populated non-home islands, recompute derived caps), or (c) gate import behind a server-side signature/HMAC the client cannot mint. At minimum, document in SPEC that import is an unbounded trust hole and restrict it (e.g. admin-only / disabled in production).

confirmed.title: WebSocket intent channel has no per-connection/per-message rate limit — DB-write amplification DoS
confirmed.description: The @fastify/rate-limit plugin only guards HTTP routes (global 100/min in app.ts:15, auth 10/min in the encapsulated scope). The WS message handler has no throttle at all. Each inbound frame chains an `applyIntent`, and applyIntent unconditionally runs `loadAndCatchUp` (which itself calls saveSnapshot) plus, on success, another saveSnapshot — so every single client frame forces at least one Postgres write, and `pushState` after each accepted intent forces yet another load+save. A single authenticated socket can fire frames as fast as it likes; the per-connection promise chain queues them all in memory (unbounded `chain` growth) while hammering the saves row. The 64 KiB maxPayload (app.ts:37) caps frame SIZE but not frame RATE. This is introduced by the migration (ws.ts is a new file); the pre-migration client-only build had no server to flood.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a per-connection token-bucket / sliding-window limiter in the message handler (e.g. N intents per second, reject excess with an error ack and/or close the socket on sustained abuse), and bound the in-memory chain depth (drop or 429 when too many intents are queued).

confirmed.title: Successful intent can emit a spurious second `{seq:-1, ok:false}` ack when the post-intent state push fails
confirmed.description: In the message handler the ack is sent (line 114) and the intent is already durably committed by applyIntent's saveSnapshot. Only AFTER that does `await pushState(...)` run (line 119). pushState calls loadAndCatchUp + serializeWorld + socket.send; if any of those rejects (transient DB error, socket mid-close), control falls to the chain's `.catch` (line 121) which sends a SECOND frame `{ seq: -1, ok: false, error }`. The client thus receives a success ack for its real seq followed by an unrelated failure frame, while the mutation actually persisted. A client that keys retry/rollback logic off failure acks could double-apply or wrongly roll back. The failure frame's seq:-1 also can't be correlated to the originating intent.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap the post-ack `pushState` in its own try/catch that swallows/logs failures (the push is best-effort; the next periodic tick re-pushes), so a push failure never manufactures a failure ack for an intent that already succeeded.

confirmed.title: REMOTE snapshot swap orphans overlay/inspector subsystems that captured worldState by reference at mount
confirmed.description: In REMOTE mode (the new default), applyRemoteSnapshot reassigns the local `worldState`/`islandStates` `let` bindings to brand-new objects returned by deserializeWorld on every server push. But several subsystems captured the ORIGINAL worldState object by reference at mount time and never see the reassignment: mountWeatherOverlay(worldState) (main.ts:380), mountSatelliteOverlay(worldState) (385), mountAntennaOverlay(worldState) (391), and mountInspectorUi(..., { world: worldState }) (1439). These close over the boot-time object (e.g. weather-overlay.ts:112 `mountWeatherOverlay(world)` then reads `sumIslandCo2(world)` at weather-overlay.ts:148 against the captured ref). After the first server snapshot, those overlays/inspector render the boot-time state forever, never reflecting authoritative server updates (CO2/weather tints, satellite positions, antenna ranges, the building inspector's live numbers). The ocean/island/fog layers escape this because rebuildWorldLayers re-reads the live `worldState` binding at call time, but anything handed `worldState` once at mount is now stale. This is REMOTE-only: LOCAL never reassigns worldState after init, so the by-reference capture is correct there.
confirmed.evidence: applyRemoteSnapshot: `worldState = d.world; islandStates = d.islandStates; worldState.islandStates = islandStates;` (main.ts:2042-2044) reassigns new objects. Mounts that captured the old object: `const weatherOverlay = mountWeatherOverlay(worldState);` (main.ts:380), `mountSatelliteOverlay(worldState)` (385), `mountAntennaOverlay(worldState)` (391), `mountInspectorUi(reg, document.body, { world: worldState, ... })` (1439). weather-overlay.ts:112 `export function mountWeatherOverlay(world: WorldState)` closes over `world` and reads `sumIslandCo2(world)` (weather-overlay.ts:148).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Do not reassign worldState in applyRemoteSnapshot; instead mutate the existing object in place (Object.assign the live worldState with the deserialized fields, and clear+repopulate islandStates) so every subsystem's captured reference observes the update. Alternatively, pass all overlays/panels a getter (() => worldState) instead of the object, mirroring how getState/activeState closures are threaded elsewhere.

confirmed.title: applyRemoteSnapshot rebuilds and destroys all ocean/island/fog GPU textures every 1s in REMOTE
confirmed.description: applyRemoteSnapshot calls rebuildWorldLayers() unconditionally on every server state push. The server pushes a full snapshot every STATE_PUSH_INTERVAL_MS = 1000 ms (ws.ts:25/100) plus after every accepted intent (ws.ts:119). rebuildWorldLayers (main.ts:510-544) re-bakes the entire ocean + island + fog overlay from scratch and calls oldOcean.destroy({ children: true, texture: true }) — a full radial-gradient sprite re-bake and GPU texture create/destroy cycle. In LOCAL this only runs on actual discovery/vision changes (gated by `needRebuild` at main.ts:2318). In REMOTE it runs at a steady 1 Hz at idle regardless of whether anything visible changed, churning the GPU and re-baking gradient textures for the whole world once per second forever.
confirmed.evidence: applyRemoteSnapshot ends with unconditional `rebuildWorldLayers();` (main.ts:2080). rebuildWorldLayers: `oldOcean.destroy({ children: true, texture: true });` (main.ts:541). Server cadence: `export const STATE_PUSH_INTERVAL_MS = 1000;` and `setInterval(() => { chain = chain.then(async () => { await pushState(...) }) }, pushIntervalMs)` (ws.ts:25, 100-102). Compare LOCAL ticker: `if (needRebuild) rebuildWorldLayers();` (main.ts:2318).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Gate the rebuild in applyRemoteSnapshot on an actual change: compare the new vision-sources signature (already computed at 2076 via visionSourcesSignature) and revealed-cell/island-discovery state against the prior values, and only call rebuildWorldLayers() when they differ — the same diff discipline the LOCAL ticker uses.

confirmed.title: Remote gateway promise rejections (intent timeout / socket closed) are unhandled in panel callsites
confirmed.description: The async-converted panel callsites run `void (async () => { const result = await gatewayResult; if (!result.ok) return; ... })()` with no try/catch. In REMOTE, gatewayResult comes from makeRemoteGateway.send -> client.sendIntent, which REJECTS (not resolves {ok:false}) on intent timeout (server-client.ts:156 `reject(new Error('Intent ${mySeq} timed out'))`), on socket-not-open (server-client.ts:149), and on socket close before ack (rejectAllPending at server-client.ts:131). A rejected awaited promise throws inside the void async IIFE, producing an unhandled promise rejection rather than the intended `if (!result.ok) return` no-op. WS reconnects are silent (connectGameServer is called with no onStatus at main.ts:270), so a transient drop during a player action surfaces as an uncaught rejection with no user feedback. There are 5 such IIFE blocks (main.ts:1445,1493,1515,1533,1556) plus the trade callsite at 1949.
confirmed.evidence: Callsite: `if (gatewayResult instanceof Promise) { void (async () => { const result = await gatewayResult; if (!result.ok) return; ... })(); return; }` (main.ts:1444-1456) — no catch. Reject sources: `reject(new Error('Intent ${mySeq} timed out'))` (server-client.ts:154-156), `return Promise.reject(new Error('Socket is not open'))` (server-client.ts:148-149), `rejectAllPending('Socket closed before ack')` (server-client.ts:131). makeRemoteGateway.send: `const ack = await client.sendIntent(type, payload);` (mutation-gateway.ts:528) propagates the rejection.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap each `await gatewayResult` in try/catch (treating a thrown error like `!result.ok`), or have makeRemoteGateway.send catch sendIntent rejections and convert them to `{ ok: false, error }` so the gateway contract is always a resolved GatewayResult and the `if (!result.ok) return` guard works as written.

confirmed.title: Auto-import of local save can re-fire and brick boot if import latency exceeds the 1s server push interval
confirmed.description: In bootRemoteClient, when the first state push is null (no server game yet) the onState handler calls importLocalSaveOrCreate() with no in-flight guard and gotFirst stays false. The server pushes a fresh (still-null) snapshot every 1000 ms until a game exists (ws.ts:100). If importLocalSaveOrCreate takes longer than 1s (large IDB save -> serializeWorld -> POST /api/game/import over a slow link), a second null push arrives while gotFirst is still false and fires importLocalSaveOrCreate AGAIN. The second run sees the server now has a save (the first import committed), so POST /api/game/import returns 409 -> r.ok is false -> it falls through to POST /api/game/new, which also returns 409 (hasSave check, routes.ts:16) -> `throw new Error('POST /api/game/new failed: 409 ...')` -> .catch(reject) rejects the bootRemoteClient promise. main()'s only handler is `main().catch(err => console.error(...))` (main.ts:2838), so boot dies with a blank screen even though the import actually succeeded.
confirmed.evidence: No guard: `} else { importLocalSaveOrCreate().catch(reject); }` (main.ts:260-263); gotFirst only set true on a non-null snapshot (main.ts:251-252). importLocalSaveOrCreate fallthrough: `const r = await fetch('/api/game/new', ...); if (!r.ok) throw new Error('POST /api/game/new failed: ...')` (main.ts:240-241). Server 409 on existing save: `if (await hasSave(pool, userId)) return reply.code(409)...` (routes.ts:16, 25). Top-level: `main().catch((err) => { console.error('[robot-islands] fatal:', err); });` (main.ts:2838-2840).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a one-shot guard around importLocalSaveOrCreate (e.g. `let importStarted = false;` set on first entry) so subsequent null pushes are ignored while the import is in flight; and treat a 409 from /api/game/new as success-equivalent (a game now exists) rather than a fatal throw, since it means a concurrent import already created the game.

confirmed.title: Auth error body rendered as raw JSON to the user instead of the message
confirmed.description: mountAuthScreen displays the auth endpoint's error response via `const text = await response.text(); errorEl.textContent = text || ...`. The server auth routes return JSON error bodies, e.g. reply.code(401).send({ error: 'invalid email or password' }) (server/src/auth/routes.ts:57) and reply.code(409).send({ error: 'email already registered' }) (routes.ts:40). So a failed login literally shows the string `{"error":"invalid email or password"}` (braces, quotes, key) in the error banner instead of a clean message. This is the primary error surface on the new auth screen and the default boot path goes through it.
confirmed.evidence: auth-ui.ts:170-172 `} else { const text = await response.text(); errorEl.textContent = text || `Request failed (${response.status})`; }`. Server response: `if (!user || !ok) return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'invalid email or password' });` (server/src/auth/routes.ts:57).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Parse the body as JSON and prefer the `error` field: e.g. `const body = await response.json().catch(() => null); errorEl.textContent = (body && typeof body.error === 'string' ? body.error : null) ?? `Request failed (${response.status})`;`

confirmed.title: REMOTE snapshot swap orphans every panel that captured world/islandStates by reference — panels render permanently-frozen data
confirmed.description: applyRemoteSnapshot reassigns the module-level world/state vars to brand-new objects returned by deserializeWorld (`worldState = d.world; islandStates = d.islandStates;`). But orbital-ui, drones-ui, routes-ui, settlement-ui, settings-ui and inspector-ui are all mounted ONCE with `world: worldState` / `islandStates` passed BY VALUE (captured object reference), and are never re-bound or re-mounted. The server pushes a full snapshot immediately on connect, after every accepted intent, AND every 1000ms (commit ba6f284), so the first push (~within 1s of boot) detaches all these panels: their `deps.world` / `deps.islandStates` point at the original, now-orphaned world object that the REMOTE client never mutates (the economy ticker is gated behind `if (!isRemote)`). Result in the new default REMOTE mode: the routes list, drone fleet, satellite telemetry, settlement targets, and inspector universe-editor all render frozen boot-time data forever. The HUD/island bar update fine because they read through the `activeState()`/`activeSpec()` getters which re-resolve the live module vars — masking the bug in casual testing and in the test suite (which never drives a snapshot swap against a mounted panel).
confirmed.evidence: main.ts applyRemoteSnapshot: `worldState = d.world;\n    islandStates = d.islandStates;` (lines 2042-2043) — reassignment, not in-place mutation. Panels mounted by reference: `mountOrbitalUi(..., { world: worldState, islandStates, ...})` (1673), `mountDronesUi(..., { world: worldState, ...})` (1735), `mountRoutesUi(..., { world: worldState, islandStates, ...})` (1801), `mountSettlementUi(..., { world: worldState, islandStates, ...})` (1817). drones-ui reads captured ref: `for (const d of deps.world.drones)` (drones-ui.ts:894); routes-ui: `for (const r of deps.world.routes)` (routes-ui.ts:1020); orbital-ui: `deps.world.satellites.length` (orbital-ui.ts:537). applyRemoteSnapshot contains no panel re-bind / remount.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Do NOT reassign worldState/islandStates in applyRemoteSnapshot. Mutate the existing objects in place (clear and repopulate `worldState.islands`, `worldState.routes`, `worldState.drones`, `worldState.satellites`, … and the islandStates Map) exactly as islandSpecsById is already cleared+repopulated in place a few lines below — so the references the panels captured stay live. Alternatively give every panel a `getWorld()`/`getIslandStates()` getter instead of a captured value, mirroring the activeState() pattern.

confirmed.title: Placement/relocate commit leaves placement mode armed during the remote round-trip — rapid clicks double-dispatch
confirmed.description: On a left-click in placement mode, main.ts calls placementUi.attemptCommit() (main.ts:811). In REMOTE the land/relocate commit path fires the intent via `deps.gateway.placeBuilding(...)` (a Promise), returns `{ ok: false }` synchronously, and only calls cancel() (which exits placement mode) inside the async success callback. There is NO in-flight guard on the land/relocate path (the ocean path uses ++beginEpoch, but that only guards anchor-pick staleness, not the commit). So between the click and the server ack, placement mode stays active and a second click fires a second place-building intent — at a different cursor tile the player has since moved to, or a duplicate. The server's legality check will reject a true duplicate on an occupied tile, but two distinct tiles both get placed when the player intended one, and the cost is charged twice server-side.
confirmed.evidence: placement-ui.ts land commit: `if (gatewayResult instanceof Promise) { void (async () => { const result = await gatewayResult; if (!result.ok) { recordRejection(); return; } cancel(); deps.onPlaced(); })(); return { ok: false }; }` (lines ~816-823) — cancel() only inside the async closure. main.ts click handler: `if (accumDrag < CLICK_DRAG_PX_MAX && placementUi.isActive()) { ... placementUi.attemptCommit(); return; }` (main.ts:803-812) — isActive() stays true until the async cancel() lands. Same shape in orbital-ui launchSatellite (orbital-ui.ts:283) and settlement-ui dispatch (settlement-ui.ts:1118).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Set an in-flight flag (or bump beginEpoch) before dispatching the placement intent and have attemptCommit early-return while a commit is pending; clear it in both the success and failure async branches. Apply the same guard to the orbital launch and settlement dispatch async paths.

confirmed.title: Trade reject in REMOTE mutates island state directly, bypassing the gateway; trade accept silently no-ops
confirmed.description: makeRemoteGateway.acceptTrade returns `{ ok: false, error: 'not supported yet' }`, so in REMOTE the accept handler returns early and trade acceptance is a silent no-op (no toast, no error). The reject callback, however, still writes `st.tradeAcceptCount += 1` and `st.tradeCooldownMs = …` directly on the local island state and prunes the offer — a client-side mutation that bypasses the gateway and will be reverted on the next server snapshot. In practice trade offers never spawn in REMOTE (tickTradeOffers is inside the `if (!isRemote)` block), so neither handler is reachable today — meaning the §trade mechanic is entirely absent for REMOTE players, which is the new default boot mode. That is a product gap introduced by the migration (the server implements no trade intent).
confirmed.evidence: mutation-gateway.ts:655 `acceptTrade() { return Promise.resolve({ ok: false, error: 'not supported yet' } ...); }`. main.ts accept handler: `const result = await gateway.acceptTrade(offer); if (!result.ok) return;` (1949-1950). main.ts reject handler mutates directly: `st.tradeAcceptCount += 1; st.tradeCooldownMs = effectiveCadenceMs(...)` with no gateway call (1967-1971). tickTradeOffers is gated: it sits inside `if (!isRemote) { ... }` (main.ts:2499+), so offers never spawn in REMOTE.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either implement trade intents server-side and route acceptTrade/reject through the gateway, or explicitly disable/hide the trade UI in REMOTE and document the gap in SPEC.md so code and spec stay in sync. At minimum, surface a 'not available' toast on accept instead of a silent no-op, and route the reject-side cooldown mutation through a gateway intent rather than mutating local state.

confirmed.title: Authoritative server economy runs advanceIsland with NO ctx — production silently diverges from the spec'd/LOCAL math (terrain recipes, modifiers, NC, lattice, cables, mirror-solar, toxicity, output-rotation all disabled)
confirmed.description: loadAndCatchUp's only production call is `advanceIsland(state, now)` with no RatesContext and no wallClockNowMs. advanceIsland/computeRates read every cross-cutting production input from ctx: ctx.terrainAt (tile-dependent recipes — a Mine on an ore/coal vein produces iron_ore baseline instead of the vein output, §8.1), ctx.worldSeed (gates advanceToxicityRolls at economy.ts:2555 AND §8.10 deterministic output rotation — both DISABLED server-side), ctx.modifierMul / ctx.ncBuff / ctx.activeBonusMul / ctx.geothermalActive / ctx.solarBoost / ctx.cableComponent / lattice inventory+caps+crossIsland. The LOCAL client passes all of these (main.ts cableLocalCtxFor builds the full ctx incl. `terrainAt: spec?.terrainAt`). So the SERVER (the authoritative owner in the new REMOTE default) computes materially different inventory/production than the spec defines and than the LOCAL client produces for the identical world. SPEC Appendix C claims 'The server reuses the client's pure layer unchanged ... runs advanceIsland for every populated island' — but the call is not the same call, so authoritative state drifts from §2.7/§3.5/§8.1/§8.10/§9.6/§13.3/§14.3. Green suite misses it: server runtime.test.ts only asserts inventory grows, never that production equals a terrain-aware/modifier-aware reference.
confirmed.evidence:
runtime.ts:27  `if (state) advanceIsland(state, now);`  (no ctx, no wallClockNowMs)
economy.ts:2555 `if (ctx?.worldSeed) { advanceToxicityRolls(...); }`  // never runs server-side
economy.ts:1343 `const recipe = resolveRecipe(def, b, terrainAt, ...)`  // terrainAt undefined ⇒ bare-defId recipe
main.ts:2157 (LOCAL ctx) `terrainAt: spec?.terrainAt,` + modifierMul/ncBuff/solarBoost/cableComponent all set
confirmed.suggestedFix: Build the same per-island RatesContext server-side that main.ts.advanceEconomy builds (at minimum worldSeed + terrainAt + modifierMul; ideally the full NC/lattice/cable/solar precompute) and pass it plus wallClockNowMs into advanceIsland. Extract the client's per-tick ctx assembly into a shared pure helper so server and client cannot drift. Add a server test that advances a world with an ore-vein Mine and asserts the vein output (not iron_ore).

confirmed.title: POST /api/game/import persists any structurally-valid client snapshot as authoritative — direct forge of xp/level/inventory, defeating the migration's anti-cheat objective
confirmed.description: The /api/game/import handler accepts a client-supplied snapshot and, after only shape/version validation (isValidSaveSnapshot) and a 'does deserializeWorld throw' check, writes it verbatim as the account's authoritative save. There is NO reachability check: a logged-in player can POST a hand-crafted snapshot with every island populated at T5, maxed level/xp, and full inventory, and the server treats it as truth. The client even has a built-in path that POSTs a locally-controlled IndexedDB save here (main.ts:223 importLocalSaveOrCreate), and LOCAL mode (the ?local opt-out) lets a player generate/edit that save freely. This directly contradicts the slice5 finding doc's central conclusion ('the client cannot forge xp/level/inventory'; doc §2) and the stated migration objective ('the server owns all state and validates every mutation by re-running the pure rules'). The intent gateway is carefully anti-cheat'd, but import is a wide-open side door around it. The only guard is hasSave (one-per-account), trivially satisfied by a fresh signup.
confirmed.evidence:
routes.ts:28 `if (!isValidSaveSnapshot(snapshot)) return reply.code(400)...`
routes.ts:34 `deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), Date.now());`  // only checks it doesn't throw
routes.ts:39 `await saveSnapshot(pool, userId, snapshot);`  // stored verbatim as authoritative
routes.test.ts:56 only test is 'imports a valid snapshot -> 201' — no god-mode-rejection test exists
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (a) remove the import endpoint and migrate local saves through a server-validated path that re-derives reachable state, or (b) gate import behind a non-player trust boundary, or (c) at minimum quarantine imported saves (flag the account, exclude from any shared/competitive surface). If kept for the one-time LOCAL→REMOTE migration, restrict it to migrate-once-per-NEW-account with an explicit server-side sanity bound (e.g. reject xp/level/inventory beyond what the snapshot's lastTick could plausibly yield). Update the slice5 doc — its 'cannot forge state' conclusion is false while this endpoint exists.

confirmed.title: REMOTE ocean-platform placement is completely broken — gateway maps it to place-building, whose server validatePlacement rejects every oceanPlacement def with 'def-is-ocean'
confirmed.description: The client gateway has only one placement method (placeBuilding) and it sends anchorIslandId/terrainTarget/terrainShotMs in the payload (mutation-gateway.ts:542-544). But the server 'place-building' intent (a) destructures only {islandId,defId,x,y,rotation} — dropping anchorIslandId/terrainTarget/terrainShotMs — and (b) runs validatePlacement, which bails FIRST with {ok:false,reason:'def-is-ocean'} for ANY def with oceanPlacement===true (placement.ts:270). So in REMOTE mode (the new default boot) the entire §4 Task-10 ocean-platform mechanic is unplaceable: the server rejects it outright. Land generic-storage cargoLabel and terraformer terrainTarget are likewise dropped, so those place with wrong defaults (iron_ore label / no terraform target). No test exercises ocean placement through the WS path, so the green suite misses it.
confirmed.evidence:
mutation-gateway.ts:542 `anchorIslandId: options.anchorIslandId,` (sent)
intents.ts:189 `const { islandId, defId, x, y, rotation } = payload;` (anchor/terrain ignored)
intents.ts:208 `const v = validatePlacement(spec, state, typedDefId, x, y, rot);`
placement.ts:270 `if (def.oceanPlacement === true) { return { ok: false, reason: 'def-is-ocean' }; }`
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a dedicated ocean-placement intent (or extend place-building to read anchorIslandId and route oceanPlacement defs through the ocean validator + placeBuilding's anchorIslandId param), and pass cargoLabel/terrainTarget/terrainShotMs through to placeBuilding. Add WS-path tests for ocean platform, cargo-labeled storage, and terraformer placement.

confirmed.title: REMOTE dispatch-drone drops waypoints and selectedTier — T5 path-drawn drones become straight-line default-tier drones server-side
confirmed.description: The client gateway sends waypoints and selectedTier in the dispatch-drone payload (mutation-gateway.ts:572-583), and the pure dispatchDrone consumes both (drones.ts:416 waypoints, :423 selectedTier; isPathDrawn = waypoints.length>=2 IS the T5 mechanic). But the server intent destructures only {islandId,originX,originY,dirX,dirY,fuelLoaded} and calls dispatchDrone(world, state, originX, originY, dirX, dirY, fuelLoaded, now) — omitting waypoints and selectedTier. In REMOTE mode a player who draws a multi-waypoint T5 path, or picks a cheaper T2 drone for a short hop, gets a straight-line island-tier drone instead: wrong fuel grade consumed, wrong flight path, and the §11.5 path-drawn T5 feature silently disabled. The handler comment even claims it validates 'all six numbers finite' as the full contract, masking the dropped fields.
confirmed.evidence:
mutation-gateway.ts:581-582 `waypoints,\n selectedTier,` (sent)
intents.ts:326 `const { islandId, originX, originY, dirX, dirY, fuelLoaded } = payload;`
intents.ts:338 `dispatchDrone(game.world, island.state, originX as number, ..., fuelLoaded as number, now)` (8 args, no waypoints/tier)
drones.ts:443 `const isPathDrawn = waypoints !== undefined && waypoints.length >= 2;`
confirmed.suggestedFix: Validate and forward waypoints (array of {x,y} finite pairs) and selectedTier (DroneTier|'5-path') in the dispatch-drone intent and pass them into dispatchDrone. Add a WS-path test asserting a path-drawn drone is dispatched as T5 with the supplied waypoints.

confirmed.title: Periodic WS state-push re-advances + re-persists the save every second under load — silent extra production/wear cadence and write amplification not present in LOCAL
confirmed.description: Every connected socket runs setInterval(pushState, 1000ms); pushState calls loadAndCatchUp (ws.ts:41) which advances every populated island AND writes the advanced snapshot back via saveSnapshot (runtime.ts:29) — i.e. a DB write per account per second per open connection, plus an extra write on every accepted intent's trailing pushState (ws.ts:119). Because advanceIsland integrates [lastTick, now], the result is integration-correct, but the design intends loadAndCatchUp for catch-up, not as a 1Hz persistence loop; it couples read-only state pushes to authoritative writes. Combined with finding #1 (no ctx), every one of these writes also bakes the diverged (terrain/modifier-blind) production into the durable store. Multiple tabs for one account = multiple overlapping 1s advance+write loops on the same row (serialized only within a single connection's chain, not across connections).
confirmed.evidence:
ws.ts:100 `const tickInterval = setInterval(() => { chain = chain.then(async () => { await pushState(...) }) ...}, pushIntervalMs);`
ws.ts:41 `const game = await loadAndCatchUp(pool, userId, Date.now());`
runtime.ts:29 `await saveSnapshot(pool, userId, advanced);`  // write on every push
confirmed.suggestedFix: Split read-only projection from catch-up persistence: have the periodic push compute+send state from an in-memory live game without writing, and persist on a coarser cadence (or only on intent / disconnect). Guard against concurrent connections for the same account (single live game per user) so two tabs don't run competing advance+write loops on one row.

confirmed.title: AGENTS.md still declares "No React, no backend. Pure client-side" after the server migration
confirmed.description: The 60-commit migration added a Fastify+Postgres backend under server/ and made REMOTE (server-authoritative) the DEFAULT boot mode. SPEC.md §15.6 was updated with a 'Superseded' note in this same range (commit 3e8df3f), but AGENTS.md — the primary guidance file coding agents read first — was NOT touched at all in the entire range (git diff --stat 0bf2ed2~1..HEAD -- AGENTS.md is empty). Line 7 still asserts 'No React, no backend. Pure client-side per SPEC.md §15.6.' and cites the very § that now says the opposite. The Architecture section also never mentions the server/ workspace, the REMOTE/LOCAL split, the mutation-gateway seam, or that tests now include a server project. This violates the repo's own rule (AGENTS.md 'Source of truth': 'Every change that alters behavior must update SPEC.md in the same change — code and spec move together'); the rule was honored for SPEC.md but not for AGENTS.md, leaving an authoritative doc making a flatly false claim about the architecture.
confirmed.evidence: AGENTS.md:7 — `Vite 5 + TypeScript strict + PixiJS 8 + vitest. No React, no backend. Pure client-side per \`SPEC.md\` §15.6.`  vs  SPEC.md §15.6 (updated this range): `**Superseded:** the browser client is now display + intent-sender only; the server owns all state/persistence for server accounts (REMOTE default).`  Confirmed AGENTS.md untouched: `git diff --stat 0bf2ed2~1..HEAD -- AGENTS.md` → (no output).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Update AGENTS.md §Stack to drop 'no backend / Pure client-side' and reference the server-authoritative split (point at SPEC §15.6 superseded note + Appendix C). Add a short Architecture subsection describing the server/ workspace (Fastify+Postgres, REMOTE default, LOCAL fallback, mutation-gateway, WS intent transport) so agents don't act on the stale 'pure client-side' premise.

confirmed.title: AGENTS.md documents `npm test` as hermetic, but the migration made it require a live Postgres
confirmed.description: vitest.config.ts (added this range, +26 lines) turns the root test run into a two-project config: a 'client' project and a 'server' project whose globalSetup (./server/src/test-setup.ts) connects to Postgres, runs migrations, and whose suites TRUNCATE/migrate a real database. So `npm test` (= `vitest run`) now fails outright if Postgres is not running. AGENTS.md §Commands still describes it as `npm test  # vitest run (one-shot, all tests)` with no database prerequisite, and the surrounding doc gives no hint that a DB must be up or that DATABASE_URL defaults to postgresql:///robot_islands_test. An agent following the documented command on a box without PG will see the whole suite error and mistake it for breakage.
confirmed.evidence: vitest.config.ts:12-22 — server project with `globalSetup: ['./server/src/test-setup.ts']` and `fileParallelism: false` (comment: 'Server suites truncate shared tables and migrate/drop schema, so they must not race ... the single test database'). test-setup.ts:6-9 — `const url = process.env.DATABASE_URL ?? 'postgresql:///robot_islands_test'; ... const pool = createPool(url); await runMigrations(pool);`. AGENTS.md:15 — `npm test           # vitest run (one-shot, all tests)`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Amend AGENTS.md §Commands: note that `npm test` now runs both the client and server vitest projects and the server project needs a running Postgres (DATABASE_URL defaults to postgresql:///robot_islands_test). Optionally document `npx vitest run --project client` for a DB-free client-only run.

confirmed.title: server/tsconfig.json excludes all *.test.ts, hiding 13 real strict-mode type errors in migration test code
confirmed.description: The only server typecheck command is `npm run build -w server` = `tsc --noEmit`, and server/tsconfig.json excludes `**/*.test.ts`. Vitest does not typecheck. So none of the 14 server test files added by this migration are ever typechecked — yet they live under the repo's strict discipline (strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, noUnusedLocals). Removing the test exclude and running the project's exact `tsc --noEmit` surfaces 13 genuine type errors in migration-introduced test code (possibly-undefined indexed reads, assignment to a readonly literal property, an unused import, a number|undefined passed where number expected). The 'server typecheck clean' baseline only holds because these files are excluded; this contradicts AGENTS.md §TypeScript discipline ('New code must compile clean under these'). It also means a type-level regression in server test fixtures (e.g. a wrong intent payload shape) would never be caught.
confirmed.evidence: server/tsconfig.json:18 — `"exclude": ["dist", "**/*.test.ts"]`. With that exclude removed, `tsc --noEmit` (project options) emits e.g.: `src/game/runtime.test.ts(48,9): error TS2540: Cannot assign to 'v' because it is a read-only property.` (line 48: `v23.v = 23 as SaveSnapshot['v'];` — `v` is `typeof SCHEMA_VERSION` literal/readonly), plus `src/game/intents.test.ts(24,1): error TS6133: 'ResourceId' is declared but its value is never read.`, `intents.test.ts(185,40): error TS2345: ... 'undefined' is not assignable to 'number | bigint'`, and TS18048 'possibly undefined' at intents.test.ts lines 667/1077-1087. 13 errors total; `npm run build` with the exclude in place exits 0.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either add a typecheck command that includes test files (a separate tsconfig.test.json with the same options but include of `src/**/*.test.ts`, wired into the build/CI), or fix the 13 errors and stop excluding tests. At minimum fix the readonly-`v` assignment (build via the typed snapshot rather than mutating `.v`) and the unused/undefined issues so the test fixtures match production types.

confirmed.title: server/package.json declares main: dist/index.js but there is no dist and deploy explicitly forbids one
confirmed.description: The server workspace package.json sets `"main": "dist/index.js"`, but the server is run directly from TypeScript via tsx (start = `tsx src/index.ts`, systemd ExecStart = `node --import tsx .../server/src/index.ts`), and the deploy README states there is intentionally no compiled dist (`npm run build -w server` is `tsc --noEmit`, typecheck only). The `main` field therefore points at an artifact that never exists. It's harmless to the tsx runtime but is a misleading entry point: any tool or downstream `require/import '@robot-islands/server'` would resolve to a nonexistent file, and it invites someone to assume a build step that the deploy docs deliberately omit.
confirmed.evidence: server/package.json — `"main": "dist/index.js"`, `"build": "tsc --noEmit"`, `"start": "tsx src/index.ts"`. server/deploy/README.md:7 — `npm run build -w server` ... `(typecheck only — tsc --noEmit; the unit runs the TS directly via tsx, there is no dist/)`. server/deploy/robot-islands-auth.service:19 — `ExecStart=/root/.nvm/.../node --import tsx /root/robot-islands/server/src/index.ts`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Change `main` to `src/index.ts` (matching the tsx-direct execution model) or drop the `main` field entirely since the package is `private` and never imported as a built library.

confirmed.title: place-building intent drops cargoLabel — every generic-storage building (Crate/Warehouse) is labeled iron_ore in REMOTE regardless of the player's picker selection
confirmed.description: The client cargo-label picker (cargo-label-picker.ts) lets the player choose the resource a Crate/Warehouse holds and the gateway sends it as payload.cargoLabel (mutation-gateway.ts:541). The server place-building handler destructures only {islandId,defId,x,y,rotation} and calls placeBuilding() with 7 args — no cargoLabelOverride — so the pure fn falls back to DEFAULT_CARGO_LABEL ('iron_ore', placement.ts:57). Since REMOTE is the default boot mode, the picker is cosmetic: all crates become iron_ore. User-reported ground truth; MISSED by the detection workflow.
confirmed.evidence:
intents.ts:189 const {islandId,defId,x,y,rotation}=payload; (no cargoLabel)
intents.ts:213 placeBuilding(spec,state,typedDefId,x,y,rot,makePlacedIdGenerator(spec)) — 7 args
placement.ts:735 (cargoLabelOverride ?? DEFAULT_CARGO_LABEL); placement.ts:57 DEFAULT_CARGO_LABEL='iron_ore'
LOCAL gateway forwards it (mutation-gateway.ts:245)
confirmed.suggestedFix: In the place-building handler validate cargoLabel via isValidResourceId and forward it (plus nowMs) to placeBuilding as cargoLabelOverride.

confirmed.title: place-building intent also drops terrainTarget/terrainShotMs and nowMs — terrain-modifier building placement is broken in REMOTE
confirmed.description: Same handler: the client sends terrainTarget + terrainShotMs (mutation-gateway.ts:543-544) for terrain-modifier defs (§8.9 — pays conversion cost on a chosen terrain) and nowMs; the server forwards none of them to placeBuilding (params 8/11/12). So terrain-modifier placement uses defaults and the upfront §8.9 conversion-cost path keyed on terrainTargetOverride never fires server-side. Third symptom of the place-building payload-drop class (alongside cargoLabel and anchorIslandId/C20).
confirmed.evidence: mutation-gateway.ts:542-545 sends anchorIslandId/terrainTarget/terrainShotMs; LOCAL gateway forwards all (mutation-gateway.ts:244-248); server handler forwards none.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Validate + forward terrainTarget (TerrainKind), terrainShotMs (finite number), nowMs to placeBuilding.

rejected.title: create-route distance is computed from constituent-merged centres but eligibility allows any populated island — route to a merged-away constituent silently uses stale geometry
rejected.why:
The claim rests on three premises, all of which are false against the actual code.

(1) "If populatedIslands() applies any filtering beyond the raw flag (e.g. excluding merged constituents)..." — populatedIslands() at src/routes-ui.ts:436-442 does literally `for (const s of deps.world.islands) { if (s.populated) out.push(s); }` and nothing else. The server handler's check `if (!fromSpec.populated)` / `if (!toSpec.populated)` (intents.ts:383-384) is semantically identical. There is zero filtering divergence; the conditional that the entire bug hinges on is false. The suggestedFix (import populatedIslands so server matches client) would be a no-op refactor — the predicates already match.

(2) "After island merging, constituent island specs can remain in world.islands referenced by id while their geometry no longer represents a routable standalone island." — There is NO island-merge logic on the server. `grep -rni "merge|constituent|absorb" server/` returns nothing; merge exists only in the client (src/island-merge.ts). The server's world.islands is never mutated by a merge, so the described stale-geometry server state is never produced.

(3) Even the client's merge does the opposite of what the claim states: src/island-merge.ts:281-282 removes the absorbed island from world.islands via `world.islands.findIndex(...)` + `splice(idx, 1)`. The constituent does NOT remain in the array, so a route could not target a merged-away constituent by id. Merge also rewrites existing routes (lines 254-257). The "stale geometry / silently uses stale centres" mechanism therefore cannot occur.

The claim is self-flagged severity:minor, confidence:low, and is purely hypothetical ("If ... applies any filtering"). Verified independently: no filtering exists, no server merge exists, and merge removes (not retains) constituents. No counterexample is constructible.

rejected.title: Trade acceptance silently no-ops in REMOTE (default boot mode) with no user feedback
rejected.why:
The claim asserts that in REMOTE mode (the default boot mode) a user can click "Accept" on a trade offer and it silently no-ops via the gateway.acceptTrade() stub. I verified each cited fact, then traced reachability of the Accept button in REMOTE — which the claim never checked.

Confirmed cited facts: src/mutation-gateway.ts:655-657 returns {ok:false,'not supported yet'}; src/remote-boot.ts:8-14 makes REMOTE the default; src/main.ts:1949-1950 does `if (!result.ok) return;` with no toast.

But the claim's scenario is unreachable. Trade offers live only in `tradeRuntime.offers`, initialized to [] at main.ts:1942. The sole code that populates it is `tickTradeOffers` (main.ts:2530, which pushes offers at trade.ts:249). That call sits INSIDE the `if (!isRemote)` block that opens at main.ts:2499 and closes at main.ts:2700 — so it never runs in REMOTE. The only other writes to tradeRuntime.offers (main.ts:1961, 1973) are filter-removals inside the accept/reject callbacks, which are themselves only reachable by clicking a rendered card.

The trade card is rendered by tradeUi.update (main.ts:2701, outside the gate, always runs) but reads from the always-empty offers list: trade-ui.ts:54-64 — when `!here && elsewhere === 0` it sets the card to display:none and returns, so the Accept button (built only when an offer exists, trade-ui.ts:84/94) is never created. Therefore in REMOTE there is no offer card and no Accept button to click; acceptTrade()'s {ok:false} stub is unreachable dead code, not a silently-failing interactive affordance. The claim's core assertion ("clicking Accept does nothing / trade broken for all default users") is false — there is nothing to click. No user-facing product gap exists. (A minor real fact remains — the stub is dead in REMOTE — but that is not the claimed bug.)

rejected.title: Open panels never repaint from server snapshots — applyRemoteSnapshot refreshes HUD + map layers but not the DOM side-panels
rejected.why:
The claim's core symptom — "an open panel shows stale state after every remote mutation until the panel is manually toggled closed/open" — is false. The DOM side-panels are refreshed unconditionally every render frame by the Pixi ticker, in REMOTE mode too, NOT only when applyRemoteSnapshot runs.

What I verified independently:

1. The factual sub-claim is correct: applyRemoteSnapshot (main.ts:2039-2081) calls only refreshRetainedRates(nowWall) and rebuildWorldLayers() — no panel refresh. True.

2. But the consequence is wrong. The ticker (app.ticker.add at main.ts:2460) has an `if (!isRemote) { ... }` block that wraps ONLY the local economy advance and closes at line 2673. The panel-refresh block runs AFTER that, outside the guard: dronesUi.refresh(now), routesUi.refresh(now), settlementUi.refresh(now), orbitalUi.refresh() (lines 2715-2718) and inspector.refresh() (line 2735) execute every frame in both LOCAL and REMOTE. routesUi.refresh re-renders from deps.world.routes (routes-ui.ts:64, 646, 1018, etc.) every call. So panels repaint ~60x/sec regardless of applyRemoteSnapshot — there is no "stale until manually toggled" state.

3. Whether the per-frame repaint shows CORRECT data after a snapshot depends on whether the panel's world reference is live. routesUi is mounted with `world: worldState` by value (main.ts:1801) and applyRemoteSnapshot does `worldState = d.world` (line 2042), swapping to a NEW object — so deps.world is stale. That is the "live references" issue, which the claim explicitly declares itself INDEPENDENT of (finding #1).

4. The claim's suggested fix is self-defeating: calling routesUi.refresh() inside applyRemoteSnapshot would re-render from the stale deps.world, NOT the new d.world, so it would not fix the described stale-panel symptom. The root cause is the stale reference (finding #1), which the claim disclaims — so this finding both mis-describes the mechanism and proposes a fix that wouldn't work for the failure it names.

parity_note: Systematic intent payload-field parity sweep: exactly place-building (drops cargoLabel/anchorIslandId/terrainTarget/terrainShotMs/nowMs) and dispatch-drone (drops waypoints/selectedTier) lack field parity; the other 27 intents match.
